record.title: Dora Otter Fleener, 1973-08-21, Interview #1 - MP3 Recording
record.subject: Latah County -- Idaho
record.occupation: Farm wife; housekeeper; author of Coming West from South Dakota
record.origin: Family moved from South Dakota in 1902
record.keywords: children childhood livestock colleges and universities families literary schools death homesteads pioneers medicine illness rural communities authors

point.title: Details of the past; head lice; kerosene lamps
point.synopsis: Remembering details of the past. Getting rid of head lice. She writes so grandchildren will know what those days were like. Kerosene lamps. People got along because they didn't know anything different.

point.title: Children knew less then; children were honest and obedient
point.synopsis: Children knew a lot less then, but outlook on life was better - knew nature. Also honest and obedient.

point.title: Wanted to dance; disappointed with college; raising livestock
point.synopsis: Dora wanted to dance, but parents wouldn't permit it. She worked out while still young. College a disappointment - girls couldn't take animal husbandry. Raising livestock for love and money.

point.title: Boys and girls were equal; school and work
point.synopsis: In responsibility and freedoms boys and girls were equal. School didn't get in the way of work.

point.title: Took butter to town; ashamed of unpainted wagon; back trouble
point.synopsis: Dora took the butter to town, and was ashamed of the unpainted wagon. The kids had to get permission to do anything, but didn't mind. There may be more back trouble now because people don't work with their backs.

point.title: Unable to use her hands; family was well off; father worked hard to get by
point.synopsis: Unable to use her hands, the memories flow in. How well off the family was compared to the neighbors. Parents wouldn't speak badly of anyone. Father worked very hard to get by, hated debt. People just wanted to get by.

point.title: Farm neighbors; reading circles; schools entertained at literaries
point.synopsis: Cooperation much stronger. Farm neighbors within walking distances. Reading circle didn't last (read Silas Mariner). Schools entertained at literaries.

point.title: Marriage and her wedding set by her mother; older sister dominated the house
point.synopsis: Mother tried to get daughters to marry highly educated men. Set Dora's wedding for her. Older sister dominated the house; Dora helped her father.

point.title: Mother wrote a book; moved to Idaho
point.synopsis: Mother wrote down her feelings in a diary in South Dakota and later a book. She was more daring about coming to Idaho, and pushed father on. Father didn't believe ridges could be farmed at first.

point.title: Dave Fleener; father-in-law crossed plains; mother and brother died of cholera; bought a Moscow town lot
point.synopsis: Dora's father-in-law, Dave Fleener, crossed plains at the age of 4. His mother and brother died of cholera on the way. In 1877, Lieuallen tried to persuade him to buy a Moscow town lot. The area of Dave's home stead is too isolated for his neighbor. Dave put up barn rafters, because he was the only unmarried man around. Dave's father was inconsiderate of him.

point.title: 1852 plains crossing; traded oxen for milk; South Idaho
point.synopsis: More about the 1852 plains crossing. The family used up all their medicine to treat other's sickness. They traded oxen for a milk cow in South Idaho. They met a brother at The Dalles.

record.description: Home life as a girl. Mother's influence on household. Exclusion of women from animal husbandry at university. Fleener family's plains crossing (1852). 8-21-73 1 hr
record.transcript: Download the PDF Index No transcript available.
record.rights: Digital format copyright: University of Idaho Library. Original Copyright: Latah County Historical Society
